claims.text: A single untrained worker making pins alone might finish twenty pins in a day, perhaps only one.
Ten workers dividing pin-making into distinct operations can produce upwards of forty-eight thousand pins in a day.
In a divided pin-making operation, one worker draws the wire, another straightens it, and a third cuts it.
The multiplication of pin output from the division of labor is a factor of hundreds, not marginal.
The multiplication of pin output from the division of labor comes from organization alone, with no new technology.
Three mechanisms drive the productivity gain from the division of labor.
A worker's dexterity increases when their task narrows.
Under the division of labor, no time is lost switching between tools and stations.
Narrowing attention onto a single operation makes workers far more likely to notice a small improvement or labor-saving device that a generalist would never see.
The division of labor is limited by the extent of the market.
A porter cannot specialize in a village because there is not enough carrying to be done there.
Specialization deepens along coasts and navigable rivers first.
Isolated regions stay poor because small markets cannot absorb specialized output.
Isolated regions are poor because their people differ from those elsewhere.
The division of labor, the same force that multiplies production, also deforms the producer.
A person whose whole life is spent performing a few simple operations has no occasion to exercise judgment.
A person whose whole life is spent performing a few simple operations generally becomes as narrow as it is possible for a human being to become.
The pin factory is not an unmixed blessing but a bargain.
The enormous material gain from the pin factory is purchased with the mental range of the workers.
Public education exists to repay the cost that the division of labor imposes on the workers' mental range.

theses.text: The pin factory shows that the division of labor is not an unmixed blessing but a bargain: enormous material gain purchased with the workers' mental range.
The division of labor multiplies output by a factor of hundreds through organization alone, with no new technology.
The division of labor is limited by the extent of the market, so specialization deepens where markets are large and isolated regions with small markets stay poor.
